speaker: interviewee
text: This was at-- in '42, in Purim time. I don't know exactly what day it is, February. People, like I told you-- I was working straight three days and three nights to cut stones for the sugar factory and not to go home and know that they should take me away. And the lunchtime, both with us working and the rabbi's son. His son, his 10-year-old boy who used to come 12 o'clock and bring him lunch to eat. The third day, he comes in, this little boy of 10 years old. He doesn't bring him lunch. He tells him something. And the boy was running away from the sugar factory, down the river, and down to the city. I came back to the Jewish-- I says, something, he knows something. Let's run away from here. My father-in-law said, go and look for him and what he's doing and then let us know. I followed him, even in his house, and then into my house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my father says-- my mother says, why are you coming here? They're looking for young people. Matter of fact, your name was given. Go back the sugar factory. I said, something's fishy. The rabbi's son ran away.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm coming back to the-- going back to the sugar factory. But I'm afraid to go in. I'm staying outside. All of a sudden, I see a woman coming and breaking her hand and saying, they took away my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then there was a man. He couldn't speak-- what do you call this? He was a deaf man. He ran out. So I knew in the shoe factory, something was wrong. I didn't-- never went back there, never back. I came back to the rabbi. And I says, you're a rabbi.  You're supposed to tell us everything what's going on. And I'm-- so I called him. My father was there, and all the Jews. Why didn't you tell us that they were catching Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me-- it was winter time, they took me in the barn, and they hide me. I go down, and there are four or five people there-- Jewish people. And I am there. They hide me there. And then I-- in the evening, 4 or 5 o'clock in the evening, I hear my-- Hannah, my sister, crying. They took my father away from the sugar factory. Everybody from the sugar factory. Maybe about 200 people, they took away. It didn't help if they work or don't work. They took them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm staying in that barn underneath over there. 10 o'clock at night, my father came into the barn down to us. I says, where did you come in? He said, there was one Ukrainian guy-- a policeman, he knew him well. When they came and arrest him, he says, they are not looking for you. Your name is Icak Skaletzky. In the paper was Icak Berg Skaletzky.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was true his name was Icak Gerg, but he liked my father. He let him go. 10 o'clock at night, he came in in the same time. At that time, it took him-- they took him two, three, four days. 450 people they gathered together, they sent away to kill, too. That was Purim time. But at that time, we did not know-- till we came in [NON-ENGLISH] time. [NON-ENGLISH] time, their-- was hearing-- hearing was not so good, not so good. But then you worked in the sugar factory. We came in. We're tired, we slept. All of a sudden I hear knocking, knocking in the walls. So half-naked, only with pants, I take a-- I went out to take a look. And it's SS, next door neighbor arriving, wants to go into the house. So I'm coming back in my house. And I said SS is looking for-- and I run away to the garden. My father ran away, too. When I went to the garden, German-- German soldiers, shooting straight at us. Don't ask questions, nothing-- straight shooting. I laid down on the ground, and the dirt from the-- came into my face. So the German thought I'm dead, but I was not dead. They didn't kill me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went up, he says, what? You're alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then a young German came in, too, and took me, my father, and another boy. And he said, up-- this was [NON-ENGLISH]-- up, going up. And with sticks, beating us up. He couldn't even think about it. Of course, I was the youngest one, and the fittest one, I didn't get too much beating up. My father was-- had a beating up. We went upstairs, upstairs from the river, up, and the [NON-ENGLISH] street. Here I see a lot of people sitting on the floor. On one side men, on the other side women. And the other ways, they beat us up. Everybody had to sit down. We are sitting there. This was from 4 o'clock, I'm sitting till 8 o'clock. And more people coming, more people. Separate men, separate women. They are sitting. Next to me was [NON-ENGLISH], a Jewish guy. He was belonging to Judenrat, with a big arm band. I says, they're going to kill us. Let's pick ourselves up and run. Wherever you're going to run, your going to be safe. It's safe. He says, no, they're going to kill us. And he shows the arm band. And he didn't let me do that, and he didn't say. Be quiet, he said.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was sitting. What do I know? 19-Year-old boy. I'm sitting there.

speaker: interviewee
text: 8 o'clock, a German came in. And there was the boarding engineer, [NON-ENGLISH]. And they don't let him sit. He stays there. They're boarding a doctor, he stays there. They're boarding a baker, he stays there, with his son. I see something is-- picking up some people. A German passes by. I says to him, I'm a specialist, too. I work in the stone mine. And he comes in and feels my muscles. He says, yeah, you can work. And I was the fifth one. Stay again. Collect maybe about 50 people. They took us next door in a yard. Lay down. And that time they collected 120 men, and then they collected the nicest girls-- six of the nicest girls. Lay down, too. And then they came back at 9 o'clock-- the rest of them, pick themselves up. There were six straight, like a column from soldiers. And each one has Germans, with dogs. Go. In the-- in that time, and they went to form [NON-ENGLISH] that day was killed 2,500 people. Whoever cannot go, they had the red cross, they put them in, in the machines, and they took them in. I remember one time, one was had a stroke. They took him in the machine. One guy had a broken leg, they took him in the machine. There was another doctor-- so one says, save him. The other says, no. One came in, the German says, no, we don't save him. They put him in, too. They took him away. And we stayed there in that-- in that yard till 5 o'clock in the afternoon. 5 o'clock he called up, he said, it was no-- nobody stood against him. He says, you can go away, and you can live another four months. You can go home. And from now on, it's going to be a ghetto. And he tell us which streets were going to be a ghetto. He gave us three days to go from out-- wherever we have houses, over there, are going to be a ghetto. And we had to [INAUDIBLE] from-- this was on Thursday, Friday-- Sunday was the first day. From Sunday we had a ghetto. We had to stay in the ghetto. In that time was killed 2,500 people. We knew already, arrived 12 o'clock a day. 12 o'clock came, a full horse and wagon with clothing. And they took from us, the people-- the 120 men-- they took some of them to unload. So they came in, they say, we  have just unloaded the clothing from the people that were killed. Because that's what we knew already, 12 o'clock that day, that they were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was worse. I don't know exactly what that's going to be. Either in April or May.

speaker: interviewer
text: The year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the ghetto. And I was back-- I found out that when you work for Germans, then you're more sure than when you work for the sugar factory. So I went to the Germans, and I worked for the Germans to break up stones. My father went to the sugar factory again. I went to-- they gave me a job to drill holes. I have to put ammunition there and take them apart. I was working there. Of course, it was very hard and a dangerous job. All of a sudden, came in a German who asked, who could make for us-- we had horses. We want to have for winter the horses should be warm. The barn-- to put the barn around with special cement. I says I can do it. I took with me another guy, and I was working on the barn a month time. Another Jewish guy brought us sand. He brought us dirt. And I had to go-- to make warm for winter, to the barn. And I was staying there till September. And a Jewish comes and he brings us sand. He says, It's no use to work here. Russian prisoners-- they were in our town-- and instead of going to work in the stone mine, they took them away to the graves to make bigger the graves. That means they're going to kill the Jews. I threw away my tools. And on the way to [NON-ENGLISH], and I tell them this thing. Then came a letter from the next town-- that-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- they are killing the same day. This was [NON-ENGLISH]-- they killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, all by myself, I'm running to sugar factory to my father to tell him the story. I came to the sugar factory, and I tell my father. I tell the rest of them. Everybody run away. My father asked me, you ran away barefooted, nothing else. We're going away. I says, I didn't think about it. So my father goes back to the ghetto to get some clothing, get some food. I'm running over to the other side of the river. And there was a digger, a big hole on the other side. Some guy told me to there-- you could hide there in that ditch.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went there in that ditch, and my father knew about that ditch. And he collect clothing, and he collect food. And he came back at night to the ditch. And we stayed there in the ditch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next day, Friday, quiet-- Thursday was it-- quiet. I said, what happened? It's quiet. We don't hear shooting, nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the evening coming, the guy that belonged to him, the ditch-- and I tell him the story, and I beg him, if you go see on Friday, 8 o'clock in the morning, if it's nice and quiet there, you let us know. We will go back to and work for the Germans. And otherwise they're going to beat us up. He comes in Friday, 8 o'clock in the morning. He said, Jews going around in the streets, going to work, and nice and quiet. I pick myself up, me and my father, with all of the clothing, going back to the ghetto. And pass-- I have to go the main street. In going to the ghetto, I have a stomach ache. I went into the school, for me. And I said to father, you go in the ghetto, I'll go to the washroom, and I will be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I walk back from the washroom, a woman comes. She said, where are you going? She recognized I'm Jewish. They are catching Jews. Run. So instead of going to the ghetto, I'm running back to the beach. And I think that my father would be there, too. And I come into the ditch. My father was not there. I stay in the ditch in the evening. My father didn't show up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the Christian guy came in and said, you cannot stay here. You have to go away from here. I said, OK. Tomorrow morning, I'm running away from here. Tomorrow morning, I'm going to run away to a monastery, a mission-like-- women, about four miles from there. And I'm hiding. There was a little wood there. One woman find me, and she says, here, they're going to catch you. Go in the attic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went in the attic, and I said, Give me some food. And I stayed there for a couple days.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was no use to go. I'm always thinking, I'm going to go with the Polish people. Maybe they have guns. I have-- I have some clothing. I'm going to give them, he's going to send me-- a Polish guy, what he-- so I went there to a Polish guy-- [NON-ENGLISH]. I been there. He told me, I don't have guns. I will give you food, but you cannot stay either there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went back to [NON-ENGLISH], to that guy that had made the ditch. He told me that if I give him some money, and if I give him some clothing, he has a gun-- but he doesn't have any ammunition. I didn't want to take it that time. Now I said, going to be a gun without the ammunition.

speaker: interviewee
text: I go in, and I give him my stuff. He never gave me the gun. He never give me the gun. And I stayed there. A day-- Then he started telling me stories. You know, when originally there were 1,500 Jews left. And when they collect, they collect only about 200 or 300. The rest run away. And every day, they catch more Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that Polish guy tells me there was a guy, [NON-ENGLISH], very educated. He had the [NON-ENGLISH], printing shop. He was working for the Ukrainian. But they came in after him, so he went on the roof-- behind the roof, and he hanged himself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then three more guys working repairing tractors. They came, police, for them. They came in.  They took two. The third one went in the attic, and he hanged himself. And there are all kind of stories, and me-- a guy like that. So I'm going-- I'm going to do the same thing. I had what I had left. The clothing didn't match, but I had two belts. So I still had in my mind, I will fall down from the barn and hurt myself. I want to be dead. So I'm trying the belt in the barn. I want to hang himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I hear a Jewish voice. Who is there? And I was half mind-- I thought, it's my father. I said, Father! Where are you? And I called to a human being, and I'm kissing him. And he talks to me Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I'm not your father. Be quiet. He was another Jewish guy from our town hiding in the same place. So he saved me the life. And I told him, you saved my life. He says, this here never can relate. Let's try to find it out. And I stayed with him-- I stayed with him a couple days. But we didn't have no food. We have to go back to the town. I know people. Over there, I didn't know what-- they're going to give us some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm going back to that monastery, to the woman, saying maybe she's going to give us maybe some food.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm coming there. They're staying in the morning. A woman comes in. Oh, police is looking for Someone that saw us-- and tell, they called.  The police is looking for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm running out. It's a little bit bushes there. Nowhere to go. I drop my package, and I went in a tree. I went in the tree, and I'm saying there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I see the policeman going with the gun, looking. Oh, he left his package. But he didn't see me there. He goes around. He goes around. He couldn't find me. To the end, he comes in to pick up a package. He looks-- he picks up his head, and he sees me in the tree. He puts the gun right there straight to me. And he says, go down. I was so scared. You know, between the branches. I drop down. The sole stayed there. Tear from my-- one shoe full, the other shoe by the sole stayed there, didn't fall down. He said, put your hands on the back of your head. I put them down. Turn around. Turn around, fine. I thought that they're going to shoot me right there. Then the other-- the other Jewish guy, in the same tree, starts shaking. Oh, another one, says the policeman. He goes 5 meters in the other side, to catch him, the other one. So he went away from me about 5 meters. I start running away-- and run away. But I left the package. I had one shirt, one pair of pants. The package there, and I run away. But they didn't-- he was ashamed that he let me go. He let the other go, too, because in Christmastime, they caught the other guy in a mill. He was hiding in a mill what never worked-- the mill. So that's how I know that that time, they didn't catch the guy. He was Wolfe. They didn't catch that guy. There you go now. One shirt-- you have one shirt, one pair of pants, and it was October, already. I'm going next door to my neighbor. He should give me a jacket.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm coming in. My neighbor lays on the couch. And I see a jacket-- Chanel from a Russian prisoner. You know, the coats? I says to him-- we were talking-- I says, oh, I need it that jacket. I'm-- October, and I'm only one shirt, because I lost my package there.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you don't need anything. You're-- you're going to get killed anyway. I was kind of mad. This was a neighbor. All the years we grew up together. I used to steal [NON-ENGLISH], sweet rolls, every Friday to give to him. And here I needed-- Matter of fact, he had this other stuff from the house. He had it. We give it to him. On top of that, when they took us to the place, he took away a lot of stuff, he-- a lot of stuff. And then, the [NON-ENGLISH] should be given away anyway. But he didn't find a place, for me a coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I tell him that. He picked himself up-- he says, he has a toothache. And he walks out. So I'm walking with him outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: He caught me from the clap. He holds me like that. And he start hollering at me. You talk-- you talk like I'm a thief. You are the biggest thief in the world. You shouldn't be here, damn it-- you should leave here. I said, be quiet. A block from there, there was a prison camp. They're going to hear you. It was at 10 o'clock at night. He hollers more higher.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, well, now. Now I'm thinking. How can I go away? He holds me like that. So, I'm a human being. I know that if I pull, he's not going to let go. But if I go down, the two hands are going to stay up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what I did. I ran down. And I run away. He started hollering, Help! Police! Skaltezky's here! He told his neighbor. The neighbors came out. He said that I came in. And I ran away that time from him. I ran away back to the ditch. He didn't know about the ditch. And I stayed there in the ditch. The guy doesn't let me die-- I have no food. I was thinking I'm going to go to a woman, what I-- I knew her. She lives 4 or 5 miles there. Maybe she's going to save me there. Maybe she's going to give me some food. The next day, I'm going to that woman. I cannot ask her name. If I ask her name, she would know of where I go. So I asked a different name until I came in. I came into that house. The woman saw me here. She says, come in the house. I said, I talked to myself, it's good. She let me go in the house. I didn't see a house for six, seven weeks. I stayed in the house there. She says, I have a Jewish girl, Ethel-- my neighbor, a Jewish girl-- I-- I'm hiding her. And she-- and the girl came out. A beautiful girl, the best in the world. And she was so hunted. And a farmer wanted to take her and to save her. No, she should be a wife for him. She ran away from him. She didn't want to be a wife to him. And she ran away from him. And I stayed with the [INAUDIBLE]. And I stayed with that woman. She didn't give us much food. Every couple days, I'm going at night begging a piece of bread. And when I come back, I have maybe 10, 15 pieces of bread. I give her half, for me half. But overnight, I'm so hungry. I'm eating up. The next night, I want to go again. She says, you're going to-- they're going to catch you. She gave me back a piece of a bread. And she keeps me for another two days. She don't let me go. Then it was very cold. That woman-- her husband went to-- was in the-- in the military. The Russians mobilized him. She needs wood. So she gives-- she find a man, and she's like a girlfriend to him. And he's going to come and going to give wood and going to give some other.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, she says, what are you going to do? he's going to stay here overnight. Where are-- where you going to be? I think and think-- what am I going to do now?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the farm there was an oven-- a round oven, and then a step going up. A step and then going on top of dome-- other side over there, you warm up yourself. I said, these steps is ground. I'm going to take out the whole step, the whole thing, and I'm going to make it a false step, and I'm going to hide there. It took me three days to dig out under the oven. And the step I put in. And then the air on top, by everybody, they keep-- they make bread every week. So they keep it there from week to week, and I-- and to leave a piece of bread there-- the flour. You know, like yeast. I made an artificial top, and then I put that stuff here. And then when he comes in, me and the girl went into that place. And we stayed until the man goes away-- 3, 4 o'clock. Sometimes all night if he's staying-- in that little step underneath there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I stayed with her maybe six weeks. All of a sudden she says. I cannot keep you. Take your girl and go away. The girl was crying. She made water. And I said to her, you know what? I'm going to go away at night, maybe she going to leave you. Because she was-- she was-- she was doing everything for her. She was in-- with clothes, she was doing-- maybe she would keep you. So I ran away that night, I ran away from her.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went away. I was going to ask-- it was another Christian guy, a very-- a very famous, smart guy. I'm asking about him. And I came into him. He had a Russian prisoner. He told me about my father. That, like I told you. I-- in the ghetto, we made a special place to hide, if something's wrong. So when the father came in, and it was surrounded with Germans, he went in the hiding place. My father, my wife's father, and the uncle and aunt-- and they all four went into that place, and they were hiding for a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that Russian guy told us, five months they sit. After eight days, somebody was-- find him. And they took him out. And they took to the fortress, [NON-ENGLISH], and he was killed there on the fortress ground. That's why I know about my father, that he was in that place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, like I says--
